articles.title: IVS Q2 — Engagement
content: Is the elder currently engaged in work, household responsibilities, social participation, or routine activities?

ul: **Actively engaged (0)** — regular engagement in work, household roles, social participation, caregiving, or routine activities
**Limited engagement (1)** — participates occasionally; reduced activity levels
**Rarely engaged (2)** — very limited engagement in routine or social activity
**Completely disengaged (3)** — largely disengaged from routine or meaningful activities

content: Score **2 or 3** → proceed to EVA.

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: Regularly engages in work, household roles, social participation, caregiving, or routine activities.
Very limited engagement in routine or social activity.
Largely disengaged from routine or meaningful activities.

articles.title: IVS Q3a — Visible health concern
content: Visible health or medical concern?

ul: **No (0)** — no visible health/medical concern
**Mild (1)** — minor visible concern; not significantly affecting functioning
**Moderate (2)** — visible health concern affecting wellbeing or functioning
**Severe (3)** — severe visible health vulnerability or distress

content: Score **1, 2, or 3** → proceed to EVA. (Precautionary principle: any visible concern triggers full assessment.)

articles.title: IVS Q3a — Operational definitions
content: v3 bundled health/emotional/caregiving into one Q3. v4 splits them into Q3a/b/c so each domain is rated independently — improves inter-rater reliability significantly.

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: Minor visible concern; not significantly affecting functioning.
Visible health concern affecting wellbeing or functioning.
Severe visible health vulnerability or distress.

articles.title: IVS Q3b — Visible emotional distress or isolation
content: Visible emotional distress or isolation?

ul: **No (0)** — no visible emotional distress or isolation
**Mild (1)** — minor emotional distress or social withdrawal
**Moderate (2)** — visible distress affecting wellbeing
**Severe (3)** — severe psychological distress or social isolation

content: Score **1, 2, or 3** → proceed to EVA.

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: No visible emotional distress or isolation.
Minor emotional distress or social withdrawal.
Visible emotional distress or isolation affecting wellbeing.
Severe psychological distress or social isolation.

articles.title: IVS Q3c — Visible safety, caregiving, or dependency concern
content: Visible safety, caregiving, or dependency concern?

ul: **No (0)** — no visible safety, caregiving, or dependency concern
**Mild (1)** — minor concern; not significantly affecting elder's situation
**Moderate (2)** — visible caregiving or dependency concern
**Severe (3)** — severe caregiving inadequacy, abandonment, or dependency crisis

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: No visible safety, caregiving, or dependency concern.
Minor concern; not significantly affecting elder's situation.
Severe caregiving inadequacy, abandonment, or dependency crisis.

articles.title: IVS Q4a — Hospitalisation in past 6 months
content: Hospitalisation in past 6 months?

ul: **No (0)** — no hospitalisation in past 6 months
**Once (1)** — one hospitalisation in past 6 months
**Multiple (2)** — more than one hospitalisation
**Prolonged or repeated (3)** — prolonged hospitalisation or repeated admissions affecting condition

content: Score **1, 2, or 3** → proceed to EVA. Any hospitalisation in 6 months is a vulnerability signal.

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: Prolonged hospitalisation or repeated admissions affecting condition.

articles.title: IVS Q4b — Fall or fracture in past 6 months
content: Fall or fracture in past 6 months?

ul: **No (0)** — no falls or fractures
**One (1)** — one fall (no fracture) in past 6 months
**Multiple (2)** — multiple falls in past 6 months
**Severe (3)** — fall with fracture, hospitalisation, or significant injury

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: One fall (no fracture) in past 6 months.
Fall with fracture, hospitalisation, or significant injury.

articles.title: IVS Q4c — New or worsening dependency
content: New or worsening dependency in past 6 months?

ul: **No (0)** — no new dependency identified
**Mild (1)** — some new dependency but managing with existing support
**Moderate (2)** — significant new dependency requiring additional support
**Severe (3)** — severe new dependency or caregiving collapse

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: Some new dependency but managing with existing support.
Significant new dependency requiring additional support.
Severe new dependency or caregiving collapse.

articles.title: IVS Q5 — Dependable support
content: Does the elder currently have dependable support available when needed for daily life or emergencies?

ul: **Consistently available (0)** — dependable support regularly available for routine, emergencies, illness
**Occasional gaps (1)** — support generally available but occasional delays or absence
**Limited or unreliable (2)** — support exists but inconsistent or insufficient
**No dependable support (3)** — no reliable support system despite visible need

content: Score **2 or 3** → proceed to EVA.

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: Dependable support regularly available for routine needs, emergencies, illness, or crises.
Support generally available but occasional delays or absence.
Support exists but is inconsistent, unreliable, or insufficient.
No reliable or dependable support system despite visible need.

articles.title: Section D — Category Assignment
content: Convert the weighted EVA total into a vulnerability category. Any critical risk flag overrides the score to **Profound Need** regardless of total.

Downgrade from severe categories (High or Profound) requires reassessment + documentation + supervisor validation. This prevents underestimation of severe vulnerability and protects against premature de-escalation.

articles.title: Section E1 — Immediate Action Protocol
content: If any critical risk flag in Section C is ticked, the mandatory action from Guidelines §11.1 must be initiated and recorded here.

head: Field
Detail
rows: CO / Sup / City Lead per protocol

content: Each entry here MUST also be entered as a care-plan task in Section E2 with corresponding priority. Failure to act within timeframe is reviewed by the supervisor at the next weekly meeting.

articles.title: Section E2 — Care Plan Tasks
content: One row per task generated from this assessment. Status updated at each subsequent visit. **The reassessment opens this section first** — review each open task before starting a fresh EVA. This is what makes the tool longitudinal.

head: Field
Values
rows: Entitlement / Health-NCD / Health-referral / Health-mental / Mobility aid / Nutrition / Caregiver support / Safety / Other
Not started / In progress / Stuck (with reason) / Done / Closed

ul: **In progress** — documents being gathered  /  submitted, awaiting response
**Stuck** — missing document  /  portal issue  /  elder unable/unwilling  /  family resistance  /  awaiting PHC referral
**Done** — benefit received  /  linkage confirmed
**Closed** — not pursued  /  elder deceased / migrated

articles.title: Section E3 — Reassessment Cadence
content: Auto-derived from Section D category. Same cadence table as Section D.

Regardless of cadence, immediate reassessment is triggered by: fall, hospitalisation, death of caregiver, widowhood, violence/abuse, visible deterioration, severe distress, new dependency, food insecurity, unsafe shelter, major illness diagnosis, displacement.

articles.title: Section E4 — Supervisor Escalation
content: **Required** when:

ul: Category is High or Profound
Any critical-risk flag is ticked
Abuse, safety, or caregiver-collapse signals during the visit
Yes / No
If yes — reason + to whom (Sup / City Lead)

articles.title: Section E5 — Additional Observations
content: Free text. Anything not captured above: family dynamics, community context, CO's own reflections.

content.quote: Use this space for the things that don't fit a structured field but matter to understanding this elder's situation. Six months later, this is the prose that reminds you of the texture of the case.

content: Sensitive observations (abuse, violence, financial exploitation, family conflict) go in the **restricted file** — not here.
